title: Outflank, Then Push
setup: King and pawn versus king, with the white king already in front of its pawn — but Black holds the **direct opposition**.
hint: Don't push the pawn yet. **Side-step** the king to break the opposition first, then drive forward.
explanation:
## Key Principle: Opposition

With the black king directly in front of yours, pushing the pawn too soon leads to stalemate or loss of the pawn. The right idea is to **outflank**: move the king sideways so the enemy king has to give way.

## The Plan

1. **{{Kd6}}** — White steps to the queenside, threatening to invade on d7 or e7. Black is forced into the only defence, **{{Kd8}}**, taking the opposition again.
2. With the kings still facing off, the pawn is now **safe to advance**. {{e6}} reaches a square defended by the king and shoulders the black king back.
3. After ...Ke8 (or ...Kc8) White will play **e7** and shepherd the pawn home.

> The key idea: in K+P vs K, the king travels first and the pawn comes later.

## What Goes Wrong If You Push Too Early

If instead White plays an immediate 1.e6?, Black answers 1...Kf8! and after 2.Kf6 Ke8 3.e7 the position is **stalemate** — Black has no legal move and the game is drawn. The lesson: in pawn endings, **the king leads and the pawn follows**.
